abstract: Grounded in the Reformed theological principle of *Sola Scriptura*, this article examines the current crisis within youth ministry, specifically its struggle to cultivate lasting Christian maturity in young people. A central driver of this inefficiency is the increasing isolation of the youth from the adult world, particularly from their own parents and the broader congregational community. To address this, the author proposes a family-oriented model of youth ministry that emphasizes the primary responsibility of parents in their children's spiritual formation. Within this framework, the church’s role shifts toward equipping and supporting families in their discipleship journey. By identifying key stakeholders and reimagining their respective roles, this study offers fresh perspectives on fostering a more integrated and effective approach to ministry.
